startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 60-year-old hypertensive man is brought to the emergency department after sudden collapse. He appears conscious but cannot move or speak. Work through this case exploring pontine anatomy and clinical correlations.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified locked-in syndrome and demonstrated strong knowledge of pontine anatomy, cranial nerve relationships, and cerebellar connections.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review pontine divisions, facial colliculus formation, and cerebellar peduncles.
Revisit the basis pontis vs tegmentum, CN VI-VII relationship, locked-in syndrome features, and middle cerebellar peduncle anatomy.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 60-year-old man with uncontrolled hypertension is brought to the ED after sudden collapse. On examination, he is awake and alert — his eyes are open and he appears to follow commands — but he is quadriplegic and anarthric (cannot speak). He can only communicate through vertical eye movements and blinking.

An MRI reveals a massive pontine haemorrhage. This is locked-in syndrome: the patient is fully conscious but the ventral pons (basis pontis) containing the corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts is destroyed.

The pons is divided into a ventral part called the basis pontis and a dorsal part called the tegmentum.

branchingQuestion.question: In locked-in syndrome from pontine haemorrhage, the patient is fully conscious but can only communicate through vertical eye movements. Why is consciousness preserved?

alternatives.text: The reticular activating system in the tegmentum (dorsal pons) is spared, preserving consciousness
The cerebral cortex is directly damaged but the brainstem compensates

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: The cerebral cortex is intact in locked-in syndrome. Consciousness is preserved because the tegmentum (dorsal pons) containing the reticular activating system is spared.

alternatives.text: The cerebellum takes over consciousness functions when the pons is damaged

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The cerebellum does not mediate consciousness. In locked-in syndrome, consciousness is preserved because the tegmentum (containing the reticular activating system) is spared while the ventral basis pontis is destroyed.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! The tegmentum (dorsal pons) containing the reticular activating system is spared.

On the MRI, you identify key pontine structures. The facial nerve (CN VII) fibres loop over the abducens (CN VI) nucleus to form the genu of CN VII, creating the facial colliculus — a small elevation on the floor of the 4th ventricle.

The middle cerebellar peduncle is the largest peduncle and carries pontocerebellar fibres from the pontine nuclei to the cerebellum, forming part of the corticopontocerebellar pathway.

branchingQuestion.question: The facial colliculus is a landmark on the 4th ventricle floor. How is it formed?

alternatives.text: CN VII fibres loop over the CN VI nucleus, creating an elevation called the facial colliculus
CN VI fibres loop over the CN VII nucleus to form the colliculus

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: It's the reverse — CN VII (facial) fibres loop around the CN VI (abducens) nucleus, not the other way round. This looping creates the genu of CN VII and the facial colliculus.

alternatives.text: The trigeminal nucleus (CN V) creates the colliculus as it projects into the 4th ventricle

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The trigeminal nucleus does not form the facial colliculus. It is created by CN VII fibres looping over the CN VI nucleus on the floor of the 4th ventricle.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Key pontine anatomy:

  • Pontine divisions: Ventral basis pontis (motor tracts) + dorsal tegmentum (reticular formation, cranial nerve nuclei)
  • Locked-in syndrome: Conscious but quadriplegic; communicates via vertical eye movements only
  • Facial colliculus: CN VII fibres loop over CN VI nucleus on 4th ventricle floor
  • Middle cerebellar peduncle: Largest peduncle; carries pontocerebellar fibres to the cerebellum
